learning_objectives: Define user experience and its core components
Trace the history of UX from human factors to modern digital design
Distinguish UX design from UI design and graphic design
readings: Don't Make Me Think by Steve Krug, Ch. 1-2
The Design of Everyday Things by Don Norman, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to User Experience Design. This course lays the foundation for understanding how people interact with products and how we can design experiences that are useful, usable, and delightful.
key_concepts: Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb: useful, usable, desirable, findable, accessible, credible, valuable
History of human-computer interaction and the emergence of UX as a discipline
The role of a UX designer in a product team
UX as a process, not just a deliverable
Stakeholders in the UX process: users, business, engineering
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of three well-known apps comparing their UX approaches — analyzing onboarding flows in Duolingo, Slack, and a poorly designed municipal website

review.value: Framework by Peter Morville defining seven facets of user experience: useful, usable, desirable, findable, accessible, credible, valuable
UX encompasses the entire user journey and emotional response; UI focuses specifically on visual layout and interactive elements

learning_objectives: Explain the concept of mental models and how they shape user expectations
Identify cognitive biases that affect user behavior
Apply basic principles of cognitive psychology to design decisions
readings: The Design of Everyday Things by Don Norman, Ch. 2-3
About Face by Alan Cooper, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Every user arrives with existing expectations about how a product should work. Understanding mental models is the first step toward designing intuitive interfaces.
key_concepts: Mental models and conceptual models
Norman's gulfs of execution and evaluation
Affordances, signifiers, and constraints
Recognition vs. recall in interface design
Cognitive load theory and its implications for design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive exercise: students sketch how they think a microwave interface works, then compare mental models across the class
setup_requirements: Access to Miro or FigJam for collaborative exercises

review.value: Internal representations users build about how a system works based on prior experience, expectations, and cultural context
The gap between what a user wants to do and what the interface allows them to figure out how to do

learning_objectives: Describe the phases of a typical UX design process
Compare waterfall, agile, and lean UX approaches
Identify appropriate methods for each design phase
readings: Lean UX by Jeff Gothelf, Ch. 1-3
Interaction Design: Beyond Human-Computer Interaction, Ch. 9

lecture_notes.introduction: UX design follows a structured process from research through evaluation. Understanding when and how to apply different methods is key to effective practice.
key_concepts: Double Diamond framework: Discover, Define, Develop, Deliver
Lean UX cycles: Think, Make, Check
UX in agile development sprints
Design thinking as a problem-solving approach
Iterative design and the value of failing fast
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study walkthrough of a real redesign project showing deliverables at each phase from user research through usability testing
setup_requirements: Figma account for collaborative design work
Access to course case study materials on LMS

review.value: Design framework with four phases — Discover (research), Define (synthesis), Develop (ideation), Deliver (implementation) — alternating between divergent and convergent thinking
Approach that minimizes waste by using rapid experimentation cycles of hypothesis, prototype, and validation

learning_objectives: Apply Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics to evaluate an interface
Identify common usability problems using heuristic analysis
readings: Nielsen Norman Group: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design
Don't Make Me Think by Steve Krug, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Heuristic evaluation is one of the fastest and most cost-effective methods for identifying usability problems. Learning to apply established principles systematically is a core UX skill.
key_concepts: Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics in detail
Severity ratings for usability problems (0-4 scale)
Conducting a heuristic evaluation: process and documentation
Limitations of expert review vs. user testing
Shneiderman's Eight Golden Rules of Interface Design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live heuristic evaluation of a student-selected website, documenting findings with severity ratings and screenshots

review.value: The system should always keep users informed about what is going on through appropriate feedback within reasonable time
0 = not a problem, 1 = cosmetic, 2 = minor, 3 = major, 4 = catastrophic — guides prioritization of fixes

learning_objectives: Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative research methods
Plan and conduct a basic user interview
Synthesize interview findings into actionable insights
readings: Interviewing Users by Steve Portigal, Ch. 1-4
Just Enough Research by Erika Hall, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Good design starts with understanding real users. User research provides the evidence base that separates assumption-driven design from user-centered design.
key_concepts: Qualitative vs. quantitative research and when to use each
Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured interviews
Writing effective interview questions: open-ended, non-leading
Active listening techniques for UX researchers
Affinity diagramming to synthesize qualitative data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live mock interview with a volunteer, followed by class discussion analyzing the interview technique and findings
setup_requirements: Audio recording app for practice interviews
Sticky notes or Miro board for affinity diagramming

review.value: Interview format with a prepared guide of open-ended questions but flexibility to follow interesting threads — the most common UX research interview format
Bottom-up synthesis method where individual observations are grouped into clusters to reveal patterns and themes

learning_objectives: Create evidence-based personas from research data
Write realistic usage scenarios that capture user goals
Map a user journey showing touchpoints, emotions, and pain points
readings: About Face by Alan Cooper, Ch. 5-6
Mapping Experiences by Jim Kalbach, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Personas and journey maps transform raw research data into actionable design tools that keep the team focused on real user needs throughout the design process.
key_concepts: Data-driven personas vs. assumption-based proto-personas
Persona components: demographics, goals, frustrations, behaviors, context
Scenario writing: connecting personas to design problems
Customer journey mapping: stages, actions, thoughts, emotions, opportunities
Jobs-to-be-done framework as an alternative to personas
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a persona and journey map from provided research data set, using Figma templates
setup_requirements: Research data packet for in-class exercise

review.value: A fictional but research-grounded character representing a user segment, including goals, behaviors, frustrations, and context of use
A visualization of the end-to-end experience a user has with a product or service, showing stages, actions, thoughts, emotions, and opportunities for improvement

questions.question: Explain the difference between an affordance and a signifier, providing one real-world example of each.
questions.answer: An affordance is a property of an object that determines how it can be used (a flat plate on a door affords pushing). A signifier is a perceivable indicator that communicates how something should be used (a 'Push' label on the door). Affordances exist whether or not the user perceives them; signifiers are deliberate communication aids.

rubric.key: Explains the distinction between the two concepts

questions.question: Name and briefly describe three of Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics, and provide an example of a violation for one of them.
questions.answer: Examples: (1) Visibility of system status — the system should always keep users informed (violation: a file upload with no progress indicator). (2) Match between system and real world — use familiar language and concepts (violation: error messages showing raw database codes). (3) User control and freedom — provide undo and exit options (violation: a multi-step form with no back button).

questions.question: Compare the Double Diamond framework with Lean UX. When would you choose one approach over the other?
questions.answer: The Double Diamond (Discover, Define, Develop, Deliver) is a comprehensive framework suited for complex problems requiring deep research and exploration. Lean UX (Think, Make, Check) emphasizes rapid iteration and validated learning, ideal for agile teams working on incremental improvements. Choose Double Diamond when the problem space is unclear and requires significant upfront research; choose Lean UX when working within established products where quick experimentation can validate hypotheses.

rubric.key: Meaningful comparison with context for choosing each

questions.question: You are conducting user interviews for a new budgeting app. Write two open-ended, non-leading interview questions that would help you understand users' current financial management habits.
questions.answer: Example questions: (1) 'Walk me through how you currently keep track of your spending during a typical month.' (2) 'Tell me about a time when you felt surprised or frustrated by your financial situation — what happened and what did you do?' These are open-ended (not yes/no), non-leading (don't suggest an answer), and focus on actual behavior and past experiences rather than hypothetical preferences.

rubric.key: Both questions are open-ended, not yes/no
Questions target actual behavior or past experience
Questions are relevant to understanding financial habits

major_assignments.title: Heuristic Evaluation Report
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive heuristic evaluation of a live website or mobile app using Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics. Document all findings with severity ratings, screenshots, and actionable redesign recommendations.
objectives: Apply all 10 of Nielsen's usability heuristics systematically
Document usability issues with evidence and severity ratings
Communicate findings in a professional report format
deliverables: Written report (8-10 pages) documenting findings organized by heuristic
Annotated screenshots for each identified issue
Severity rating matrix summarizing all findings
Executive summary with top 5 prioritized recommendations

criteria: All 10 heuristics systematically applied
Issues correctly categorized under appropriate heuristics
Severity ratings are justified and consistent
Analysis demonstrates deep understanding of each heuristic
Clear, annotated screenshots for every issue
Specific descriptions of where and how each issue occurs
Real user impact clearly articulated
Multiple examples provided for each issue category
Recommendations are specific and actionable
Solutions align with the identified problems
Feasibility and priority are considered
Recommendations reference established design patterns
Professional formatting and organization
Clear writing free of jargon misuse
Proper citation of heuristics and sources
Executive summary is concise and compelling
Thorough application of all 10 heuristics with accurate categorization
High-quality evidence with annotated screenshots and clear descriptions
Actionable and prioritized recommendations grounded in design principles
Professional report presentation and clear communication

weekly_quizzes.description: Biweekly quizzes assessing understanding of UX concepts, principles, and terminology

class_participation.description: Active participation in critiques, discussions, and in-class exercises

heuristic_evaluation.description: Comprehensive heuristic evaluation report of a live product

design_exercises.description: Weekly hands-on exercises including persona creation, journey mapping, and sketching

final_portfolio.description: Curated portfolio of all course deliverables demonstrating growth in UX thinking

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction. In-class exercises cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Extensions must be requested at least 48 hours in advance with a valid reason.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and peer feedback are encouraged. All written deliverables must be individual work. Cite any sources including classmate ideas that influenced your analysis.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism of any kind, including uncredited use of AI-generated text in reports, will result in a zero for the assignment and referral to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with Figma (free education account), a modern web browser, and access to Miro or FigJam for collaborative exercises.
